statement: The Cherokee Nation District Court serves all 14 counties of the Cherokee Nation Reservation and handles civil, criminal, and juvenile cases. The court is located at 17675 S. Muskogee Ave, 2nd Floor, Tahlequah, OK 74464.
source_title: Cherokee Nation District Court - Official Page
quote: The District Court of the Cherokee Nation serves all 14 counties of the Cherokee Nation Reservation. The court handles civil, criminal, and juvenile cases. The civil realm can hear matters of name changes, protective orders, and juvenile guardianship. The criminal cases are those that arise from the reservation and are prosecuted by the Cherokee Nation Attorney General's Office.

statement: Cherokee Nation Court Clerk's Office can be reached at (918) 207-3900 for general court information.
source_title: Cherokee Nation Courts - Official Website
quote: If you have any questions or concerns, please contact the Court Clerk's Office at 918-207-3900.

statement: The Cherokee Nation Marshal Service provides one dedicated Marshal that serves as a court bailiff and process server on a regular basis.
source_title: Cherokee Nation Supreme Court and District Court Report 2020
quote: The Marshal service provides one dedicated Marshal that serves as a court bailiff and process server on a regular basis, but additional...

statement: Cherokee Nation Supreme Court contact is cnsupremecourt@cherokee.org, phone (918) 207-3900, Court Clerk: P.O. Box 1097 Tahlequah, OK 74465.
source_title: Cherokee Nation Supreme Court - Official Page
quote: For more information on the Supreme Court, please call (918) 207-3900 or email at cnsupremecourt@cherokee.org. Court Clerk: P.O. Box 1097 Tahlequah, Ok 74465.

statement: Service procedures not publicly available on Cherokee Nation website for general process serving. Contact the Court Clerk's Office directly at (918) 207-3900 for specific service requirements.
source_title: Cherokee Nation Courts - Official Website
quote: Service procedures not publicly available — contact tribal court clerk directly.

statement: The Choctaw Nation Code of Civil Procedure Section 51 defines process as any document used by the court to acquire or exercise jurisdiction over a person or specific property. All process shall be under the seal of the court clerk, signed by the court clerk, and dated.
source_title: Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma Code of Civil Procedure
quote: The style of all Process shall be 'The Choctaw Nation to:' and all process shall be under the seal of the court clerk and shall be signed by the court clerk, and dated the day it is issued.

statement: The Choctaw Nation Code of Civil Procedure Section 52 allows the court or judge to appoint a substitute for service of process. A person appointed has the same power as tribal police.
source_title: Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma Code of Civil Procedure - Section 52
quote: The court or judge thereof, or the clerk in the absence of the judge upon his oral or written order, for good cause, may appoint a person to serve a particular process or order, who shall have the same power to execute it which the tribal police have.

statement: Tribal police or process server must endorse upon every summons the day and hour it was received (Section 53). Penalties for failure to execute or return process may be up to $1,000 (Section 54).
source_title: Choctaw Nation Code of Civil Procedure - Sections 53-54
quote: The tribal police officer or the process server shall endorse upon every summons, order of arrest... the day and hour it was received by him... if he fail to do so... he shall be amerced by the court in a sum not exceeding One Thousand Dollars($1,000.00)

statement: Choctaw Nation District Court has locations in Durant (2250 Chukka Hina, 580-920-7027), Talihina (302 Church Street, 918-567-3582), and McAlester (1133 S. George Nigh Expressway, 918-426-7654).
source_title: Contact Choctaw Nation Judicial Branch
quote: Choctaw Nation Tribal Courthouse. 302 Church Street. Talihina, OK 74571. 918-567-3582. Choctaw Nation Judicial Center. 2250 Chukka Hina. Durant, OK 74701. 580-920-7027. Choctaw Nation Judicial Center. 1133 S. George Nigh Expressway. McAlester, OK 74501. 918-426-7654.

statement: Choctaw Nation Judicial Branch provides e-filing portal for electronic filing of court documents.
source_title: Choctaw Nation E-Filing Portal
quote: Get 24/7 access to electronically filed documents, receive proof of delivery, and instantly download file-stamped copies of your filings.

statement: Choctaw Nation service of process may be performed by tribal law enforcement officers, persons licensed to serve process by any state or federal government, or persons specially appointed by the court.
source_title: Choctaw Nation Code of Civil Procedure
quote: Process, summons, subpoenas and service... A tribal law enforcement officer... A person licensed to serve process in civil cases by the Choctaw Nation, any state, or the federal government... A person specially appointed by the court for that purpose.

statement: The Chickasaw Nation Judicial Department is located at 821 N. Mississippi, Ada, Oklahoma 74820. The District Court phone number is (580) 235-0279 and the Supreme Court phone number is (580) 235-0281.
source_title: Chickasaw Nation Judicial Department - Official Website
quote: Judicial Building. 821 N. Mississippi. Ada, Oklahoma 74820. (580) 235-0281. District Court. P.O. Box 129. Ada, Oklahoma 74821. (580) 235-0279.

statement: The Chickasaw Nation District Court has jurisdiction over all civil actions arising under the Constitution, Laws or Treaties of the Chickasaw Nation. The District Court focuses on family issues (divorce, child custody, child support, adoption), civil matters, juvenile matters, guardianship, and criminal misdemeanors for crimes committed by Indians on tribal land.
source_title: Chickasaw Nation District Court
quote: The District Court has jurisdiction over all civil actions arising under the Constitution, Laws or Treaties of the Chickasaw Nation which arise in the tribal jurisdiction... The District Court will focus on family issues such as divorce, child custody, child support, parental rights, visitation and adoption, civil matters, juvenile matters, and guardianship.

statement: Under Chickasaw Nation code, defendants must serve an answer within 20 days after service of summons and petition. Defenses including insufficiency of process and insufficiency of service of process may be asserted by motion.
source_title: Chickasaw Nation Code - Title 06
quote: A defendant shall serve his answer within twenty (20) days after the service of summons and petition upon him... defenses may at the option of the pleader be made by motion: d. insufficiency of process; e. insufficiency of service of process;

statement: Service procedures not publicly available on Chickasaw Nation Judicial Department website for general process serving. Contact the District Court at (580) 235-0279 for specific service requirements.
source_title: Chickasaw Nation Judicial Department
quote: Service procedures not publicly available — contact tribal court clerk directly.

statement: The Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court is located at the Mound Building, 2501 Lvmhvlke (Eagle) Street, Okmulgee, OK 74447. General questions: (918) 758-1400 or assistance@mcndistrictcourt.com.
source_title: Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court - Official Website
quote: Monday – Friday | 8:00 am – 5:00 pm. Muscogee (Creek) Nation Complex Mound Building P.O. Box 652 | Okmulgee, OK 74447. Phone: 918.758.1400

statement: The Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court accepts only money orders or cashier's checks at the window - no cash payments.
source_title: Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court
quote: The District Court does not accept payment in Cash, only Money Orders or Cashier's checks are accepted at the window.

statement: The Official Court Rules for the Muscogee (Creek) Nation Court are available on the District Court website.
source_title: Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court Rules
quote: The Official Court Rules for the Muscogee (Creek) Nation Court can be found here: Rules-of-the-MCN-District-Court

statement: Service procedures not publicly available on Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court website for general process serving. Contact the court at assistance@mcndistrictcourt.com or (918) 758-1400 for specific service requirements.
source_title: Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court
quote: Service procedures not publicly available — contact tribal court clerk directly.

statement: The Seminole Nation Tribal Court is located at P.O. Box 1220, 206 E. 2nd St., Wewoka, Oklahoma 74884. Main phone: (405) 257-7200.
source_title: Seminole Nation Tribal Courts
quote: SEMINOLE NATION TRIBAL COURT. P.O BOX 1220. 206 E. 2nd St. Wewoka, Oklahoma 74884. Main Number: (405) 257-7200

statement: The Seminole Nation also maintains a Court of Indian Offenses (CFR court) located at Wewoka Agency, P.O. Box 632, Seminole, OK 74818, phone (405) 382-0091, Court Clerk: Loisetta Nix.
source_title: Oklahoma Indian Legal Services - Guide to Pro Se Form Pleadings Available in Tribal Courts
quote: SEMINOLE NATION COURT OF INDIAN OFFENSES. Wewoka Agency. P.O. Box 632. Seminole, Oklahoma 74818-0632. 405/382-0091. FAX: 405/382-0091. Court Clerk: Loisetta Nix.

statement: Service procedures not publicly available on Seminole Nation website for general process serving. Contact the tribal court directly at (405) 257-7200 for specific service requirements.
source_title: Seminole Nation of Oklahoma
quote: Service procedures not publicly available — contact tribal court clerk directly.

statement: The Quapaw Nation Court is located at Ki-Ho-Ta Center, 5681 S 630 Rd, Miami, OK 74354. Phone: 918-238-3173. Court Administrator: Corissa Millard. Court Clerk: Julio De Los Santos. Hours: Monday-Thursday 8 AM - 5 PM, Friday 8 AM - 4 PM.
source_title: Quapaw Nation Court Staff Directory
quote: Physical Address: Ki-Ho-Ta Center, 5681 S 630 Rd, Miami, OK 74354. Phone: +1 9182383173. Hours: Monday through Thursday 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. Friday 8 a.m. to 4 p.m. Staff: Millard, Corissa; De Los Santos, Julio

statement: The Quapaw Nation requires a $35 fee for initial process server license application, with $15 yearly renewal. Applicants must be at least 18, not convicted of a felony or crime of moral turpitude. Process servers must return a Return of Service form to the Court Clerk on the next business day after service.
source_title: Quapaw Nation Process Server Application
quote: A fee of $35.00 will be collected with the initial application, a Quapaw Nation Process Server License number will be assigned and ID card. Licenses are renewable yearly, with a $15.00 fee... A Return of Service form provided by this Court will be filled out and returned by the Process Server to the Court Clerk, on the next business day after service.

statement: The Quapaw Nation has a licensing requirement for private process servers under Resolution 041809-A. Court may waive background investigation if applicant presents a current process server license from any Indian tribe or current state process server license.
source_title: Quapaw Nation Process Server Application - Resolution No. 041809-A
quote: All applicants are subject to background investigation to verify the requirements for licensing, however the Court may waive this if the applicant presents current process servers license from any Indian tribe or current state process server license.

statement: Quapaw Nation court rules require that service of process on an employee of the Quapaw Tribe must be made by contacting the Human Resource Department and setting a time of service with the employee in the Human Resource office.
source_title: Quapaw Nation Process Server Application - Service Rules
quote: Service of process required on an employee of the Quapaw Tribe of Oklahoma or any of its entities, will be made by contacting the Human Resource Department and setting a time of service with the employee in the Human Resource office, so as not to interfere in the operations of said business.

statement: The Quapaw Nation has adopted Court Rules under Resolution 052023-H governing court operations, including procedures for civil procedure and service of process.
source_title: Quapaw Nation Court Codes
quote: Resolution 052023-H: Adopting Court Rules - Formal adoption of procedural rules governing court operations.

statement: The Quapaw Nation court rules provide for dismissal if summons is not issued within 6 months or if issued and not served within 6 months.
source_title: Quapaw Nation Court Rules
quote: If an action be commenced, but no summons issued within six (6) months, or if issued and not served within six (6) months, the Court may dismiss such action on its own motion and without notice.

statement: The Quapaw Nation Bar Association requires a certificate of good standing dated within 90 days. Pro Hac Vice fee is $25.00 per case filing. Annual membership fee is $40.00.
source_title: Quapaw Nation Bar Association
quote: An official, original certificate of good standing issued by the bar of the highest court of any state of the United States... dated no more the 90 calendar days prior... Pro Hac Vice fee is $25.00 per case filing... annual membership fee of $40.00 is due by January 31st.

statement: McGirt v. Oklahoma, 591 U.S. ___ (2020), held that the Muscogee (Creek) Nation's reservation was never disestablished by Congress. The reservation remains 'Indian country' for purposes of criminal jurisdiction.
source_title: McGirt v. Oklahoma, 591 U.S. (2020)
quote: The Court held that Congress had never properly disestablished the Creek Nation's reservation in eastern Oklahoma. As a result, the Court concluded that the Creek Reservation remained 'Indian country.'

statement: Following McGirt, the Oklahoma Court of Criminal Appeals extended the reasoning to recognize that the Cherokee Nation, Chickasaw Nation, Choctaw Nation, and Seminole Nation had pre-statehood reservations that Congress had never diminished or disestablished.
source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - Oklahoma Cities and Towns in Indian Country
quote: The Oklahoma Court of Criminal Appeals extended the reasoning in McGirt and held that the Cherokee Nation, Chickasaw Nation, Choctaw Nation and Seminole Nation had pre-statehood reservations that Congress had never diminished or disestablished.

statement: Under McGirt and its progeny, the state of Oklahoma lacks criminal jurisdiction over all crimes committed by or against Indians on the reservations of the Five Tribes. The state continues to exercise jurisdiction over offenses by non-Indians against non-Indians in Indian country.
source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - McGirt Impact on Criminal Jurisdiction
quote: The state of Oklahoma lacks criminal jurisdiction over all crimes committed by or against Indians on the reservations of the Five Tribes. The state continues to exercise jurisdiction over offenses by non-Indians against non-Indians in Indian country.

statement: Oklahoma v. Castro-Huerta, 597 U.S. (2022), held that the Federal Government and the State have concurrent jurisdiction to prosecute crimes committed by non-Indians against Indians in Indian country.
source_title: Oklahoma v. Castro-Huerta, 597 U.S. (2022)
quote: We conclude that the Federal Government and the State have concurrent jurisdiction to prosecute crimes committed by non-Indians against Indians in Indian country.

statement: After McGirt, the Five Tribes combined have filed in excess of 10,000 criminal cases. The Cherokee Nation went from filing less than 100 criminal cases a year pre-McGirt to almost 3,000 cases in nine months following the Hogner reservation decision.
source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - McGirt Impact on Tribal Courts
quote: The Five Tribes combined have filed in excess of 10,000 criminal cases. The Cherokee Nation went from filing less than 100 criminal cases a year pre-McGirt, to almost 3,000 cases in the nine months following its reservation decision.

statement: Following McGirt, tribal police forces, prosecutors' offices, courts, and juvenile justice programs grew at an exponential pace across all Five Tribes.
source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - Tribal Court Expansion
quote: Tribal police forces, prosecutors' offices, courts and juvenile justice programs have grown at an exponential pace. The Cherokee Nation Office of the Attorney General has gone from one full-time prosecutor to eight.

statement: McGirt and its progeny recognized the Quapaw Nation reservation was never disestablished in State v. Lawhorn, 2021 OK CR 37.
source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - Quapaw Nation Reservation
quote: The OCCA has recently extended the McGirt analysis to the Quapaw Nation, finding the tribe's reservation was never disestablished in State v. Lawhorn, 2021 OK CR 37.

statement: For service of process on tribal land, process servers must: (1) Research the specific tribal law, (2) Contact the Tribal Court Clerk and/or Tribal Police before attempting service, (3) File documents with the Tribal Court. Permission is often required to enter tribal lands for official business.
source_title: Serving Papers on Tribal Land | Oklahoma Guide
quote: Unlike in the state system, a process server generally does not have pre-granted authority to enter tribal lands to conduct official business. Permission is often required... Call the Tribal Court Clerk: This is the most direct way to get accurate, up-to-date information.

statement: Under 12 O.S. Ch. 2 App., Rule 30, a tribal judgment may be filed with any Oklahoma district court clerk and enforced in the same manner as a state district court judgment. An affidavit with names and addresses must be filed, and no enforcement action may issue for 20 days.
source_title: Oklahoma Statutes - District Courts Rule 30 - Filing of Tribal Judgments
quote: A copy of any tribal judgment authenticated in accordance with the applicable act of Congress or of the statutes of this state or the law of the tribe may be filed in the office of the district court clerk of any county of this state. The district court clerk shall treat the tribal judgment in the same manner as a judgment of the district court of any county of this state which may be enforced or satisfied in like manner.

statement: Oklahoma law enforcement cross-deputization agreements and other tribal-state agreements are maintained on the Oklahoma Secretary of State website at www.sos.ok.gov/gov/tribal.aspx.
source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - Tribal Cross-Deputization Agreements
quote: The law enforcement cross-deputization agreements (and other tribal-state agreements and compacts) are all available on the Oklahoma Secretary of State website: www.sos.ok.gov/gov/tribal.aspx.

statement: Cross-deputization agreements allow officers from one sovereign (tribal, state, local, or federal) to exercise the other's laws within specified boundaries. The legal basis includes the Indian Law Enforcement Reform Act (25 U.S.C. § 2801 et seq.) and the Oklahoma Interlocal Cooperation Act (74 O.S. § 1221 et seq.).
source_title: Cross-Deputization in Oklahoma Tribal Law | Addison Law Firm
quote: Cross-deputization agreements allow tribal and state/local officers to enforce each other's laws... The Indian Law Enforcement Reform Act (25 U.S.C. § 2801 et seq.) authorizes the Bureau of Indian Affairs to enter cooperative agreements with tribal and state agencies.

statement: The Muscogee (Creek) Nation maintains an Intergovernmental Cross-Deputization Agreement with the United States and political subdivisions of the State of Oklahoma, authorized by NCA 92-15 § 108 and 74 O.S. §§ 1221 et seq.
source_title: Muscogee (Creek) Nation Intergovernmental Cross-Deputization Agreement
quote: The Muscogee (Creek) Nation has enacted legislation authorizing the Nation to enter into cross-deputization agreements with federal, state and tribal governments pursuant to NCA 92-15 § 108; and the Legislature of the State of Oklahoma has authorized the State and its political subdivisions to enter into cooperative agreements in accordance with the Oklahoma Inter-local Cooperation Act, 74 O.S. §§ 1221 et seq.

statement: Historical cross-deputization agreement between Bureau of Indian Affairs, Muscogee (Creek) Nation, and Hughes County, Oklahoma was filed with Oklahoma Secretary of State in 1996.
source_title: Cross-Deputization Agreement among Hughes County, BIA, and Muscogee (Creek) Nation
quote: Enclosed please find a Cross-Deputization Agreement between the Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA), the Muscogee (Creek) Nation of Oklahoma, and Hughes County, Oklahoma... filed with the Secretary of State

statement: The Oklahoma History Society maintains a Tribal Compacts database listing cross-deputization agreements, intergovernmental cooperative agreements, and other tribal-state compacts dating back to 1991.
source_title: Tribal Compacts - Oklahoma Historical Society
quote: Cross-Deputization Agreement - Bureau of Indian Affairs / Ottawa County - May 21, 1992... Deputation Agreement - Chickasaw / State of Oklahoma - January 23, 2006... Tribal Addendum to Deputation Agreement - Quapaw / State of Oklahoma - May 19, 2011

statement: Post-McGirt, the Cherokee Nation marshal is not aware of a single city or town wholly or partially within the Cherokee Nation Reservation that does not have a cross-deputization agreement with the nation.
source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - Cross-Deputization Post-McGirt
quote: The Cherokee Nation marshal is not aware of a single city or town that is wholly or partially within the Cherokee Nation Reservation that does not have a cross-deputization agreement with the nation.

statement: The Quapaw Nation has a cross-deputization agreement effective March 1, 2022, and law enforcement agreements with Oklahoma Highway Patrol effective January 28, 2022.
source_title: Serving Papers on Tribal Land - Quapaw Nation Cross-Deputization
quote: Cross-deputization agreement (effective 3/1/2022), law enforcement agreements with Oklahoma Highway Patrol (1/28/2022)

statement: Following Oklahoma v. Castro-Huerta (2022), the State of Oklahoma and the federal government have concurrent jurisdiction to prosecute crimes committed by non-Indians against Indians in Indian country. However, tribes lack criminal jurisdiction to prosecute non-Indians under Oliphant v. Suquamish Tribe unless the crime falls under VAWA.
source_title: Oklahoma v. Castro-Huerta, 597 U.S. (2022)
quote: We conclude that the Federal Government and the State have concurrent jurisdiction to prosecute crimes committed by non-Indians against Indians in Indian country... Indian tribes lack criminal jurisdiction to prosecute crimes committed by non-Indians such as Castro-Huerta, even when non-Indians commit crimes against Indians in Indian country. See Oliphant v. Suquamish Tribe, 435 U.S. 191, 195 (1978).

statement: Concurrent jurisdiction can create a 'pass the buck' dynamic where federal authorities reduce involvement when state authorities are present, potentially resulting in fewer prosecutions and less public safety in Indian country.
source_title: Passing the Buck: The Perils of Oklahoma v. Castro-Huerta
quote: Concurrent jurisdiction increases the number of law enforcement agents with jurisdiction, it also creates opportunities for those law enforcement agents to shirk responsibility... concurrent jurisdiction has sometimes 'created a pass-the-buck dynamic... with the end result being fewer police and more crime.'

statement: McGirt v. Oklahoma recognized that reservations of the Five Civilized Tribes (Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Muscogee Creek, and Seminole) remain 'Indian country' as defined by 18 U.S.C. § 1151, covering approximately 19 million acres in eastern Oklahoma.
source_title: McGirt v. Oklahoma - Chief Justice Roberts Dissent
quote: Not only does the Court discover a Creek reservation that spans three million acres and includes most of the city of Tulsa, but the Court's reasoning portends that there are four more such reservations in Oklahoma. The rediscovered reservations encompass the entire eastern half of the State—19 million acres.
